summary: This guest post by Marc Joffe announces a PDF Liberation Hackathon aimed at improving tools for extracting data from PDFs. The post argues that open source modeling efforts are hindered by the high cost of cleaning data trapped in unstructured PDF formats. While tools like Tabula and DocHive exist, they have limitations for large-scale extraction tasks. The hackathon, sponsored by the Sunlight Foundation, will bring together developers to work on PDF extraction challenges and improve open source tools for data journalists and transparency advocates.
key_points: Data cleaning costs can be prohibitive for open source modeling efforts, allowing biased analytics to persist
PDFs are a major obstacle to data accessibility, trapping information that modelers and journalists need
Existing open source tools like Tabula and DocHive help but cannot handle large-scale batch processing
The hackathon will offer prizes for solutions that advance PDF data extraction capabilities
All winning entries must publish source code publicly on GitHub
money_quotes: Since open source modeling efforts are usually low dollar operations, the costs of data cleaning may prove to be prohibitive. Hence no open model – distortion and bias continue their reign.

A corporation or well funded research institution can purchase an expensive, enterprise-level ETL tool to migrate data from the PDFs into a database. But this is not much help to insurgent modelers who want to produce open source work.
If, like me, you want to submit hundreds of PDFs to a software tool, press 'Go' and see large volumes of cleanly formatted data, you are out of luck.
Open source models can bring much needed transparency to scientific research, finance, education and other fields plagued by biased, self-serving analytics.
The PDF Liberation Hackathon is going to be a great opportunity to advance the state of the art when it comes to harvesting data from public documents.
key_insight: The inaccessibility of data locked in PDFs creates a barrier that favors well-funded institutions over transparency advocates, perpetuating biased analytics in finance and public policy.
